title: Cat Swallowed Marijuana Edible: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat ate a marijuana edible, it is a medical emergency. Learn the risks, symptoms, and why immediate veterinary care is essential for recovery.
content_html:
TL;DR: Marijuana ingestion is a high-priority veterinary emergency for cats due to their extreme sensitivity to THC; seek immediate medical care and bring photos of any packaging to help identify additional toxins like xylitol or chocolate.

What is marijuana toxicity in cats and why is it dangerous?

Marijuana toxicity occurs when a cat ingests THC (tetrahydrocannabinol), the psychoactive component of the cannabis plant. While humans use it recreationally or medicinally, cats have a much higher density of cannabinoid receptors in their brains, making them extremely sensitive to the drug. Edibles are particularly dangerous because they often contain highly concentrated THC or additional toxic ingredients like chocolate, raisins, or the sweetener xylitol.

Is marijuana ingestion in cats a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Ingestion is considered a veterinary emergency.
  • Physiological Impact: Cats cannot process THC effectively, and even small amounts cause significant neurological and cardiovascular distress.
  • Immediate Action: Contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately.
  • Precaution: Do not wait for symptoms to appear, as onset is rapid and effects are long-lasting.

Why should I provide a photo of the edible packaging to the veterinarian?

  • Dosage Identification: Provides the exact milligrams of THC ingested.
  • Ingredient Screening: Helps the vet identify other life-threatening toxins like dark chocolate or birch sugar (xylitol).
  • Faster Stabilization: Allows the medical team to provide the correct life-saving treatments more quickly.
  • Triage Accuracy: Serves as a vital tool for the veterinary team to assess the severity of the situation.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of suspected marijuana toxicosis in cats, diagnosis is based on a history of exposure and typical clinical signs, including depression, ataxia, bradycardia, hypothermia, vocalization, hypersalivation, vomiting, diarrhea, urinary incontinence, seizures, and coma. While THC is difficult to detect in body fluids, urine testing at human hospitals or using an over-the-counter marijuana drug test kit early in the course of exposure may help confirm the diagnosis. Treatment consists of supportive care, including inducing emesis and administering activated charcoal if the exposure is recent and there are no contraindications. Comatose animals should be monitored for aspiration pneumonia, given IV fluids, treated for hypothermia, and rotated frequently to prevent dependent edema or decubital ulceration. Diazepam can be administered for sedation or to control seizures. Treatment and monitoring should continue until all clinical signs have resolved.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3041)

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of THC poisoning in cats?
faqs.answer: Symptoms often include extreme lethargy, loss of coordination (stumbling or 'drunken' walk), dilated pupils, slow heart rate, and involuntary leaking of urine.

faqs.question: Can I just let my cat sleep it off at home?
faqs.answer: No. Because cats are so sensitive, they can experience dangerous drops in body temperature, seizures, or respiratory issues. Professional monitoring is necessary to ensure their vitals remain stable.

faqs.question: How long do the effects of marijuana last in cats?
faqs.answer: The effects can last anywhere from 12 to 72 hours depending on the amount ingested and the cat's metabolism, which is why veterinary support is so important.
